learn.title: Why Your Solar Panel Isn't Always at Full Power
learn.intro: Solar panels have a rated capacity, but real-world output depends on weather, location, and technology. Understanding capacity factor reveals how much energy they actually generate over time.
learn.c1_text: Capacity factor measures how much energy a solar panel produces compared to its maximum potential. A 20% capacity factor means it generates 20% of its rated power annually. This accounts for cloudy days and maintenance downtime.
learn.c2_text: Even the most efficient panels lose energy due to heat, dust, and suboptimal angles. For example, a 22% efficient panel might only achieve 15% in practice due to environmental factors.
learn.c3_text: India’s solar parks achieve ~18% capacity factors due to sunny weather but face seasonal dust and grid limitations. The country aims for 500 GW renewable energy by 2030, requiring advanced monitoring.
learn.readLabel: Read at US Energy Info Admin

learn.title: Perovskite: The Solar Breakthrough Coming Soon
learn.intro: Perovskite solar cells use a mineral-based material to achieve higher efficiency than silicon. They’re cheaper and more flexible but face durability challenges. This could revolutionize solar energy.
learn.c1_text: Perovskite cells absorb light better than silicon. Lab prototypes hit 33% efficiency, compared to silicon’s 22%. They’re also thinner and can be printed like ink.
learn.c2_text: Combining perovskite and silicon in tandem cells boosts efficiency to 30%. This layered approach captures more sunlight across different wavelengths.
learn.c3_text: India’s CSIR is testing perovskite modules for rooftops. If stabilized, they could cut solar costs by 30%, aligning with PM e-DRIVE’s goal of 100% electric mobility by 2030.

learn.title: India’s EV Revolution: Policy Powering Change
learn.intro: India’s PM e-DRIVE scheme offers subsidies, tax breaks, and charging infrastructure to boost electric vehicles. State policies add incentives tailored to local needs.
learn.c1_text: The scheme offers up to ₹1.5 lakh subsidies for EVs, waived registration fees, and free charging for 3 years. It targets 30% EV penetration by 2030.
learn.c2_text: States like Delhi offer additional subsidies and EV-specific parking. Tamil Nadu provides free electricity for charging stations, accelerating adoption.
learn.c3_text: India has 2.6 million EVs (2023), up from 1 million in 2021. The FAME II policy aims for 7 million EVs by 2025, reducing oil imports by 1 million barrels/day.

learn.title: Hydrogen from Water: PEM vs. Alkaline
learn.intro: Green hydrogen splits water into hydrogen and oxygen using renewable energy. PEM (Proton Exchange Membrane) electrolyzers are efficient but costly, while alkaline systems are cheaper but slower.
learn.c1_text: PEM electrolyzers use a polymer membrane to split water at 1.5 V. They operate at 60-80% efficiency and handle variable renewable input, ideal for solar/wind.
learn.c2_text: Alkaline electrolyzers use a liquid electrolyte at 1.8 V. They’re durable and cost ~30% less than PEM but have lower efficiency (50-70%) and slower response times.
learn.c3_text: India plans 100 GW renewable-powered electrolyzers by 2030. PEM tech is prioritized for industrial use, while alkaline systems target rural hydrogen production.
learn.stat3: India’s green hydrogen target: 5 million tonnes/year by 2030

learn.title: Virtual Power Plants: AI Managing Millions
learn.intro: Virtual power plants (VPPs) aggregate distributed energy sources (solar, batteries) and use AI to balance supply and demand in real time, stabilizing grids.
learn.c1_text: VPPs use machine learning to predict energy use and optimize storage. For example, 10,000 homes with 5 kW batteries can act as a 50 MW virtual plant.
learn.c2_text: VPPs respond to grid frequency changes in milliseconds. They participate in energy markets, earning revenue by selling excess power during peaks.
learn.c3_text: India’s 10 GW battery storage target by 2025 could enable VPPs. States like Gujarat are piloting AI-driven VPPs to manage solar variability.
learn.stat3: India’s battery storage target: 10 GW by 2025

learn.title: Solar Panels as Building Materials
learn.intro: Building Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV) embed solar cells into roofs, windows, and walls, turning structures into energy generators. This saves space and blends aesthetics with function.
learn.c1_text: BIPV replaces traditional materials like glass or steel. A solar roof tile generates power while protecting the building, cutting installation costs by 20%.
learn.c2_text: Flexible perovskite BIPV can curve around roofs or wrap windows. They’re lightweight (5 kg/m²) and durable, with 15% efficiency in lab tests.
learn.c3_text: India’s first BIPV project in Mumbai uses solar glass for office buildings. The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) offers 40% subsidy for BIPV installations.
learn.stat4: India’s MNRE BIPV target: 5 GW by 2025
learn.readLabel: Read at US Dept of Energy
